entry.title: How AI and Machine Learning Are Quietly Transforming SAP Integrations — My Perspective as a Beginner
entry.content:
The Gap I Noticed in Traditional Integration
In a typical iFlow I built recently, I had to manually define rules like:
∙ If amount is greater than 100000 — route to high value channel
∙ If region is not IN or US — mark as invalid
∙ If quantity is zero — reject the item
These rules work fine when business logic is simple and stable. But what happens when the rules keep changing? What if the threshold for “high value” changes every quarter? What if new regions get added every month? A human has to go in, update the Groovy script, redeploy, test again. That is time consuming and error prone.
This is exactly where AI and ML start making sense in SAP integrations.

 

Where AI/ML Actually Fits in SAP

1. Intelligent Data Mapping

“Mail Based Batch Processing iFlow — routing logic manually defined for each invoice type” 

One of the most frustrating parts of SAP CPI for a beginner is Message Mapping. You have to manually connect every source field to every target field. For simple scenarios this is manageable. But imagine mapping two enterprise systems with hundreds of fields each.
SAP has been working on AI assisted mapping where the system suggests field mappings based on field names, data types and historical mapping patterns. Instead of manually dragging every line, the AI proposes the mapping and you just review and approve. This saves enormous time in large integration projects.

This is my data in Postman


2. Anomaly Detection in Integration Flows

“Manually setting threshold values in Content Modifier — AI could dynamically adjust these thresholds based on patterns”

Imagine your iFlow processes 10000 invoices every day. Suddenly one day 200 invoices fail. Is that normal? Is something broken? Did a source system change its format?
ML models trained on historical message processing logs can detect these anomalies automatically. Instead of waiting for someone to notice failures in the monitor, the system alerts you proactively. SAP Integration Advisor and SAP Alert Notification Service are already moving in this direction.


3. Predictive Error Handling

“Manually defined routing conditions in SAP CPI Router — AI could learn and suggest these rules automatically based on historical data”

Traditional error handling in CPI is reactive — something fails, you get an alert, you investigate. ML changes this to proactive. By analyzing patterns in past failures — which steps fail most often, at what times, with what kind of payloads — a model can predict which messages are likely to fail before they actually do and reroute them automatically.


4. Natural Language to iFlow

“Custom business logic written manually in Groovy — imagine describing this in plain English and having AI generate it automatically” 

This one genuinely surprised me when I read about it. Researchers and SAP teams are experimenting with systems where you describe what you want in plain English — something like “receive invoices from vendor system, validate amounts, send high value ones to finance team via email” — and the system automatically generates a working iFlow.
We are not fully there yet but with large language models becoming more capable this is not science fiction anymore.


5. AI Powered Document Understanding
In my Scenario 3 assignment I processed invoices that were already structured XML. But in the real world invoices come as PDFs, scanned images, emails and all kinds of unstructured formats. SAP Document Information Extraction — part of SAP AI Business Services — uses ML to read these unstructured documents and extract fields like invoice number, vendor name, amount and date automatically. This extracted data can then flow directly into an SAP CPI iFlow for further processing.

 

What SAP Is Actually Building
SAP has been investing heavily in an umbrella platform called SAP AI Core and SAP AI Launchpad. These are the infrastructure layers that allow AI models to be trained, deployed and consumed across SAP systems.
On top of that, specific AI services have been released:
∙ SAP Document Information Extraction — extracts data from unstructured documents
∙ SAP Business Entity Recognition — identifies business entities in text
∙ SAP Translation Hub — ML powered translation for global deployments
∙ SAP Intelligent Robotic Process Automation — combines RPA with ML for smarter automation
All of these can be connected to SAP Integration Suite, meaning your iFlows can call these AI services as external APIs and enrich your integration logic with intelligence.

A Small Real World Example
Let me paint a picture. Suppose a company receives invoices from 50 different vendors. Each vendor sends invoices in a slightly different format — different field names, different structures, different currencies.
Without AI:
∙ A developer manually creates one mapping per vendor
∙ 50 vendors means 50 mappings to maintain
∙ Every time a vendor changes their format someone has to update the mapping
With AI:
∙ ML model learns the pattern of each vendor’s format from historical data
∙ Automatically adapts mappings when formats change slightly
∙ Flags only major structural changes for human review
∙ Developer effort reduces from weeks to hours

 

My Honest Take


I am still a beginner with SAP CPI. I am still figuring out XPath expressions and Groovy scripts. But even at this level I can see that the manual rule based approach has limits. The more I work with integration flows the more I appreciate why AI is being brought into this space.
The integrations of tomorrow will not just move data — they will understand it, validate it intelligently, predict problems before they happen and adapt to changes automatically. SAP is clearly building towards that future and I think it is genuinely exciting for anyone working in this space today.

References and Further Reading
∙ SAP AI Business Services on SAP Discovery Center
∙ SAP Integration Advisor Documentation
∙ SAP AI Core on SAP Help Portal
∙ SAP Community blogs on Intelligent Integration

 This blog reflects my personal observations and learning while working with SAP Integration Suite as part of my coursework. All scenarios mentioned are based on hands-on assignment work.

entry.title: Ask Me Anything with SAP Champions – Monthly Series (Session 2)
entry.content:
Hey dear SAP Community,

My name is Maria Antonieta Lopez,  I currently work as an SAP program director focusing on global SAP transformation programs, SAP S/4HANA implementations, enterprise architecture, governance frameworks, and digital innovation within the SAP ecosystem.

As an SAP Champion myself, I’ll be leading the March edition for the Ask Me Anything series, which we started together with my dear friend Sebastiano Marchesini.

So yes…

You can ask. Me. ANYTHING.

After 20+ years living inside SAP transformations, I’ve probably seen the good, the bad… and the “how did this system survive go-live?” moments. 😅

So over the next weeks I’ll be answering any question you have -- No filters - Just real SAP Live --.

💡We can explore topics like:

⚙️SAP S/4HANA implementations and global transformation programs. - How to successfully deliver end-to-end SAP S/4HANA implementations and large-scale transformation programs, enabling organizations to modernize their core business processes, standardize global operations, and adopt next-generation digital capabilities.

🏗️ SAP Enterprise Architecture. - Processes, artifacts, and strategies for TOGAF-based SAP enterprise architecture.

🧼 Clean Core in SAP Cloud ERP (Private & Public). - Project management processes for implementing or creating extensions or interfaces in your project. (BUILD for configurators and its use with Joule, pre-built APIs, newly developed APIs, and, of course, our platform-as-a-service, SAP Integration Suite, obviously within our beloved SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP).

☁️ RISE or GROW with SAP + Clean Core Governance & Methodologies. - From SAP Activate  to frameworks such as SAP Application Extension Methodology, Integration Solution Advisory Methodology, SAP Design Thinking, SAP Data Architecture Framework, and why not… even the SAP Business AI Framework.

We can talk about which format to use, or the process for something, or even… why I have to do this task.... and I'll tell you what will happen if you don't...

Muahaha! …💀 soo Yes… my SAP horror stories - like winter - are coming…

🏛️ How to build an SAP Center of Excellence (CoE) or provide an SAP AM services and actually 💙 live happily ever after with SAP 💙 using Cloud ALM + Focused Build + Activate.

🌍Globalization in a nutshell or by country. To comply locally and succeed globally... Long live SAP Document & Reporting Compliance! And our SAP partners' DRC extensions.

🧭 Solution roadmaps, etc.

And... if I don't know the answer? 👉 I promise I'll find someone who does... for you.

 

We have everything ready...  💬 Now it’s your turn.

Drop your question in the comments section 👇or You can ask anonymously here 👉 AMA Champion Anonymously Questions  or Scanning this QR 

 

This AMA edition will be open for questions until March 27, 2026.

After that, selected questions will be answered in a recorded video published on the SAP Community YouTube channel  👉https://www.youtube.com/@SAPCommunity 
A blog recap summarizing the questions and answers will also be published for the community.

Cuentame…

🤓What SAP topic should we explore together? ESPAÑOL or ENGLISH

entry.title: Modernizing integrations with event‑driven architecture and real customer use cases for SAP AEM
entry.content:
Customers want real-time business.  But many integration landscapes still rely on rigid, point-to-point connections that slow everything down.

So what’s the alternative?  Event-driven architecture.

In this upcoming session, we’ll explore how SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh (AEM) enables applications to communicate through events, creating flexible, scalable connections across SAP and non-SAP systems.

And we won’t stay theoretical.  You’ll see real customer scenarios showing how AEM helps distribute data instantly, reduce integration dependencies, and keep processes running smoothly across complex landscapes.

Plus, we’ll share practical guidance for identifying where event-driven approaches make sense in customer architectures, something partners are increasingly being asked about.

If you’re working with customers on modern integration strategies, this session is packed with insights you can immediately apply.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013314.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #EventDrivenArchitecture #ModernIntegration #SAPIntegrationSuite

entry.title: Mapping SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) Adapter to SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ Adapter
entry.content:
This blog post is part of the series Master Blog: SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite Migration – Tool and Guidance exploring how to migrate various integration patterns from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite using the Migration Tool.

Objective of this Blog

In this blog, we will focus on the mapping of SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter — with Transport Protocol “WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider” and Message Protocol both “JMS 1.x” or “JMS 2.0”) — to SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ adapter.

Since the Migration Tool skips the SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter, the approach described in this blog will help to manually configure both Sender and Receiver IBM MQ adapters in SAP Integration Suite iFlows based on source JMS (IBM MQ) SAP Process Orchestration scenarios.

Related Blogs 

  • IBM MQ JMS Adapter for SAP Integration Suite
  • Integrating IBM MQ On-Premise with SAP Cloud Integration Using the IBM MQ Adapter

SAP Process Orchestration JMS Adapter Modules

In SAP Process Orchestration, the following JMS Adapter Modules are commonly used to convert message payloads between binary and XML formats:                       

  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertJMSMessageToBinary
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertBinaryToXMBMessage
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertMessageToBinary
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/SendBinarytoXIJMSService

In SAP Process Orchestration, these modules are often required when integrating with JMS providers such as IBM MQ. However, in SAP Integration Suite they are not required, as their functionality is provided directly by the IBM MQ adapter itself. The IBM MQ adapter in SAP Integration Suite offers built-in options for message conversion, allowing users to select either byte or text message formats directly in the adapter configuration.

Variants

Based on the SAP Process Orchestration source parameters, the migration will result in three different target variants:

- Sender

  • JMS Poll
  • JMS Subscribe

- Receiver:

  • JMS

Sender

The source Sender JMS MQ channel must have the following properties:

  • Adapter Type: JMS
  • Sender
  • Transport Protocol: WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider
  • Message protocol: JMS 1.x or JMS 2.0

The relevant Sender variants — JMS Poll and JMS Subscribe — are detailed below (REST variant doesn’t apply).

JMS Poll

JMS Poll implements a message retrieval mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Sender Adapter actively polls messages from IBM MQ queues or topics at defined intervals, using a request-based approach to check for new messages periodically.

Condition

In SAP Process Orchestration, for this variant to apply, the following condition must be met:

“Use Message Listener Based Connector” checkbox in the Processing view not checked:

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

The following table shows the mapping from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite:

 

SAP Process Orchestration

SAP Process Orchestration

SAP Integration Suite

SAP Integration Suite

SAP Process Orchestration

The following SAP Process Orchestration screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Source view

If “Use SSL” checked and “WSMQ Cipher Spec” = “WebSphereMQ Cipher Spec”:

If “Use SSL” checked and “WSMQ Cipher Spec” = “Other”:

Processing view

SAP Integration Suite

The following SAP Integration Suite screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Connection view

* After maintaining the Credentials in the SAP Integration Suite Security Materials section, the alias name must be provided in the Credential Name field to establish the connection.

Processing view

If “Message Destination” = “Topic”:

If “Message Destination” = “Queue”:

 JMS Subscribe

JMS Subscribe implements a message retrieval mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Sender Adapter establishes an active subscription to IBM MQ queues or topics, enabling real-time or near real-time message delivery as soon as messages are published to the destination.

Condition

In SAP Process Orchestration, for this variant to apply, the following condition must be met:

“Use Message Listener Based Connector” checkbox in the Processing view checked:

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

Same mapping as for the JMS Poll variant, but without fields 14, 15, and 16.

Receiver

The source Receiver JMS MQ channel must have the following properties: 

  • Adapter Type: JMS
  • Receiver
  • Transport Protocol: WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider
  • Message protocol: JMS 1.x or JMS 2.0

The relevant Receiver variant — JMS — is detailed below (HTTPS variant doesn’t apply):

JMS

JMS Receiver implements a message publishing mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Receiver Adapter enables SAP Integration Suite to send messages to IBM MQ queues or topics, acting as a message producer that publishes data to designated destinations in the IBM MQ server.

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

The following table shows the mapping from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite:

 

SAP Process Orchestration

The following SAP Process Orchestration screenshots illustrate the mapping:

The following SAP Integration Suite screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Connection view

*After maintaining the Credentials in the SAP Integration Suite Security Materials section, the alias name must be provided in the Credential Name field to establish the connection.

Processing view

If “Message Destination” = “Topic”:

If “Message Destination” = “Queue”:

Advanced view

Conclusion

By following the mapping approach outlined in this blog — including detailed mapping tables and screenshots for the different target SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ sender and receiver adapter variants — users can successfully migrate their adapters from SAP Process Orchestration scenarios to SAP Integration Suite iFlows.

 * Since not all parameters can be mapped, end users must evaluate whether any functionality configured in the source SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter has not been migrated to the target SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ adapter (e.g., PI Settings, Error-Handling Settings, or Duplicate Handling).

** In addition, some functionalities — such as correlation ID mapping — cannot be mapped directly but can be configured in the SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ adapter. E.g., in the receiver adapter, it can be set dynamically using an exchange header or property.

entry.title: Orchestrating AI Assisted HR Workflows with Microsoft Copilot Using SAP BTP and SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Employees increasingly expect quick answers to questions such as:

  • What is the status of my promotion request

  • Which onboarding tasks are still pending for new hires

The objective was to allow AI-assisted HR queries while maintaining strict governance over SAP SuccessFactors data and workflows.

The organization had very clear requirements. AI tools should not have direct access to SuccessFactors APIs, and they should not execute actions using external tokens. All interactions had to remain fully controlled, auditable, and aligned with HR governance policies.

To achieve this, we designed an architecture where SAP BTP served as the orchestration and control layer between Microsoft Copilot and SuccessFactors.

When a user interacts with Copilot and asks a question related to HR processes, the request is not sent directly to SuccessFactors. Instead, it is routed to SAP Integration Suite running on SAP BTP.

At this stage, the integration layer performs several important functions. The request is validated, enriched with user context, and evaluated against access policies. This ensures that the request complies with the organization’s HR security and authorization rules.

Once validated, the request triggers a workflow through SAP Build Process Automation. This workflow layer applies business rules and approval logic related to HR processes. For example, certain HR data may only be accessible to managers or HR partners, and some actions may require additional validation steps.

Only after these governance checks are completed does the integration layer interact with SAP SuccessFactors modules such as Employee Central, onboarding workflows, or performance management services.

The requested information is retrieved in a controlled manner and transformed into a simplified response that can be returned to the AI interface.

From the user’s perspective, Copilot appears to instantly provide HR insights. However, behind the scenes the request has passed through a fully governed integration and workflow orchestration layer within SAP BTP.

This approach provided several advantages for the organization. AI could enhance the employee experience without introducing uncontrolled access to HR systems. All interactions remained traceable and compliant with internal policies. At the same time, the architecture created a scalable foundation for future AI-driven HR scenarios.

As enterprises continue integrating AI assistants into workplace tools, the key challenge will not be enabling AI itself but ensuring it operates within controlled enterprise architectures. Platforms such as SAP BTP provide the necessary capabilities to bridge modern AI services with critical business applications while maintaining governance, security, and operational visibility.

entry.title: Upload binary content via Cloud Integration to SAP with BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB
entry.content:
SAP Generic Object Services (GOS) is commonly used to store attachments for business objects such as sales orders, purchase orders, or deliveries.

In SAP PI/PO, many implementations use the RFC function module BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB to upload binary content to SAP. However, when working with SAP Cloud Integration, handling binary content requires a slightly different approach.

In this blog post, I will demonstrate how to build a simple iFlow that uploads a file from an external system to SAP and stores it as a GOS attachment using the RFC function module BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB.

Scenario Overview

The scenario works as follows:

  • A file is uploaded to an SFTP server.
  • Cloud Integration processes the file and converts it into the format required by BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB.
  • The RFC adapter calls the SAP backend system via Cloud Connector.
  • The document is stored in SAP and can be accessed via transaction OAOR.

Below is the overall iFlow design.

Processing Steps

Step 1 – Base64 Encode the File

The file content is first Base64 encoded. This prevents issues caused by special characters that may interfere with XML processing in later steps.

Step 2 – Prepare the XML Structure

Next, we prepare an XML message that will be used as the input for message mapping. 

Example structure:

<MT_File>
	<FileName>${header.CamelFileName}</FileName>
	<FileSize>${header.CamelFileLength}</FileSize>
	<Content>${body}</Content>
</MT_File>

Step 3 – Convert XML into RFC Request

The XML message is then mapped into the RFC structure required by the function module BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB. Example request:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<ns1:BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB xmlns:ns1="urn:sap-com:document:sap:rfc:functions">
	<OBJECT_KEY>0000000001</OBJECT_KEY>
	<BINARY_FLAG>X</BINARY_FLAG>
	<CLASSNAME>SOFFICEINTEGRATION</CLASSNAME>
	<CLASSTYPE>OT</CLASSTYPE>
	<COMPONENTS>
		<item>
			<DOC_COUNT>00000000</DOC_COUNT>
			<MIMETYPE>text/plain</MIMETYPE>
			<COMP_SIZE>1957</COMP_SIZE>
		</item>
	</COMPONENTS>
	<CONTENT>
		<item>
			<LINE>446965204DC3BC...E62726F74</LINE>
		</item>
		<item>
			<LINE>656E20756E...CC39FC3A4</LINE>
		</item>
	</CONTENT>
	<SIGNATURE>
		<item>
			<DOC_COUNT>00000000</DOC_COUNT>
			<PROP_NAME>BDS_DOCUMENTCLASS</PROP_NAME>
			<PROP_VALUE>TXT</PROP_VALUE>
		</item>
		<item>
			<DOC_COUNT>00000000</DOC_COUNT>
			<PROP_NAME>BDS_DOCUMENTTYPE</PROP_NAME>
			<PROP_VALUE>TXT</PROP_VALUE>
		</item>
		<item>
			<DOC_COUNT>00000000</DOC_COUNT>
			<PROP_NAME>DESCRIPTION</PROP_NAME>
			<PROP_VALUE>german_text.txt</PROP_VALUE>
		</item>
	</SIGNATURE>
</ns1:BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB>

Important Parameters 

OBJECT_KEY: The identifier of the business object. For example, for a purchase order it would be like 4500000001, for delivery it would be like 0034423362. 

BINARY_FLAG: Set to X because the request contains binary data.

CLASSNAME: Defines the GOS class for the object. For example, `EKKO` would be for pruchase order, `LIKP` would be for delivery. 

CLASSTYPE: 'BO' for business object, 'OT' for others

COMPONENTS/item/MIMETYPE: its value is determined based on file extension, for example, 

  • .txt:  text/plain
  • .pdf: application/pdf
  • .xml: application/xml

COMPONENTS/item/COMP_SIZE: this is the file size. Map its value from header CamelFileLength. 

CONTENT/item & CONTENT/item/LINE: This section contains the actual binary content of the file. The content must be split into multiple lines because the RFC structure expects the data in chunks.

Some custom UDFs are used in the message mapping to handle binary processing.

import com.sap.it.api.mapping.Output;

def String getHexValue(String arg1){
    String hex = arg1.bytes.collect { String.format("%02X", it) }.join('')
    
	return hex 
}

def String getFileExtension(String fileName){
    def extension = fileName.substring(fileName.lastIndexOf('.') + 1).toUpperCase()
	return extension 
}

def String getByteSize(String ediString){
    Integer hexSize = ediString.bytes.size()
    
	return hexSize + '' 
}

def String base64Decode(String input) {
    def base64String = input.replaceAll("\\s", "")
    def base64Decoded = Base64.decoder.decode(base64String)
    
    return new String(base64Decoded)
}

def void splitBinaryContent(String[] input, String[] interval, Output output) {
    Integer intv = interval[0].toInteger()
    String s = input[0]
    
    Integer arrayLength = (int) Math.ceil(((s.bytes.size() / (double) intv)));
    String[] result = new String[arrayLength];
    
    int j = 0;
    int lastIndex = result.length - 1;
    for (int i = 0; i < lastIndex; i++) {
        byte[] subArray = Arrays.copyOfRange(s.bytes, j, j + intv);
        result[i] = new String(subArray, "UTF-8")
        j += intv;
        output.addValue(result[i]);
    }
    
    byte[] lastSubArray = Arrays.copyOfRange(s.bytes, j, s.bytes.size());
    result[lastIndex] = new String(lastSubArray, "UTF-8");
    output.addValue(result[lastIndex]);
}

Testing the Integration

After deploying the iFlow:

  • Upload a file to an SFTP server.
  • The iFlow processes the request.
  • The RFC adapter sends the request to SAP.
  • The document is stored successfully.

The attachment can then be viewed in SAP using transaction OAOR.

Conclusion

In this blog post, we demonstrated how to upload binary files from SAP Cloud Integration to SAP using the RFC function module BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB.

Key takeaways:

  • Binary content should be Base64 encoded before XML processing.
  • The content must be converted and split into chunks for the RFC structure.
  • Custom UDFs help with encoding, size calculation, and content splitting.

This approach can be used to attach documents to various SAP business objects via GOS. If you have questions or improvements, feel free to share them in the comments.

entry.title: How to Fix Groovy Script Warnings and Upgrade to Script Version 2.x in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
What Changed in the March 15, 2026 Release

SAP introduced enhanced design guideline checks for Groovy scripts in integration flows.

These checks help developers ensure their scripts:

  • Avoid unsupported libraries

  • Avoid wildcard imports
  • Remove duplicate or unused imports
  • Use supported runtime classes
  • Remain compatible with Groovy runtime 4.0.29

The script editor now categorizes issues into:

  • Errors
  • Warnings
  • Information

A new Upgrade Readiness Check highlights script incompatibilities that could break in future runtime upgrades.

Unlike some design guideline checks, Groovy script compatibility issues cannot be skipped, meaning they must be addressed.

What I Noticed After the Update

After the March 15 release update, I reviewed one of my integration flows and noticed that the Groovy script used in my Exception Subprocess was showing warnings in the script editor.

The script itself was still working correctly and running smoothly, and there were no runtime errors in the integration flow. However, the editor flagged some upgrade readiness warnings, mainly related to wildcard imports and compatibility with future runtime upgrades.

Once I upgraded the script to Groovy Script Version 2.x, the editor automatically resolved the issues and removed the warnings, making the script fully compliant with the latest SAP design guidelines.

Example Warning: Wildcard Import

One of the warnings highlighted was:

Usage of Wildcard (*) imports on Internal Classes/Packages is not supportedand may be removed in future.

The script contained the following import:

 
import groovy.json.*;

Wildcard imports like this are now discouraged because they can introduce hidden dependencies and compatibility issues.

Fixing the Wildcard Import Issue

The solution was simple: replace the wildcard import with a specific class import.

Old Import

 

import groovy.json.*;

Fixed Import

import groovy.json.JsonBuilder

After replacing the import, the script complied with the new design guidelines.

Fix Script Incompatibilities Using the Script Editor

SAP has made it easier to fix issues directly inside the Groovy script editor.

Step 1 — Open the Groovy Script

Navigate to your integration flow and open the local Groovy script step.

Step 2 — Check the Problems View

The script editor automatically detects issues and displays them in the Problems tab.

Two major categories are shown:

Upgrade Readiness Check

Checks for:

  • Unsupported libraries
  • Deprecated classes
  • Wildcard imports
  • Duplicate imports

General Script Check

Checks general scripting practices.

Step 3 — Apply Automatic Fix

For issues that support automatic fixes:

  1. Select the issue
  2. Click Fix
  3. Confirm the suggested changes

The editor automatically updates the code.

Step 4 — Save and Deploy

After resolving the issues:

  1. Save the script
  2. Return to the integration flow
  3. Simulate or deploy the flow

Upgrading Groovy Script from Version 1.x to 2.x

Another useful feature is the direct upgrade option for Groovy scripts.

Why Upgrade to Groovy Script 2.x?

Upgrading provides several benefits:

  • Compatibility with Groovy runtime 4.0.29
  • Automatic fixes for incompatible code
  • Access to modern Groovy language features
  • Better support for future runtime upgrades

How to Upgrade the Script

  1. Open the Groovy script in the editor
  2. Click Upgrade
  3. Confirm the upgrade

All integration flow steps using this script will automatically move to Groovy Script Version 2.x.

SAP also keeps a backup of the previous script version in the Scripts folder in case you want to revert.

Real Example: Exception Subprocess Script Upgrade

In my case, the Exception Subprocess script was originally written using Groovy Script 1.x.

Old Script (Groovy Script 1.x)

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;import groovy.util.XmlSlurper;import java.util.HashMap;import groovy.json.*;import org.apache.camel.Exchange;import org.apache.camel.builder.SimpleBuilder;import groovy.xml.*import groovy.util.*;

This script included:

  • Wildcard imports
  • Older SAP integration libraries

Updated Script (Groovy Script 2.x)

After clicking Upgrade, the script was automatically updated by the editor.

 
// This is Groovy Script Version 2.x, running with Groovy runtime 4.import com.sap.it.script.v2.api.Message;import com.sap.it.script.v2.api.language.SimpleBuilder;import groovy.json.JsonBuilder

Key improvements:

  • Uses the new SAP script API
  • Removes wildcard imports
  • Compatible with Groovy runtime 4

The functionality of the script remained the same, but the code is now future-ready and compliant with SAP standards.

Recommendation

After upgrading your scripts:

  • Always simulate the integration flow
  • Verify the message processing logs
  • Ensure the output matches expected behavior

Even though SAP provides automatic fixes, validation before production deployment is recommended.

Conclusion

The March 15, 2026 SAP Integration Suite update introduced helpful improvements for maintaining clean and upgrade-ready integration scripts.

Using the Upgrade Readiness Check and the Groovy Script 2.x upgrade option, developers can easily fix incompatibilities and align their scripts with SAP best practices.

In my case, upgrading the script and fixing the wildcard import warning resolved the issues highlighted by the editor and ensured my Exception Subprocess script is ready for future SAP runtime upgrades.

entry.title: Cloud Connector 2.19.0.2 is here!
entry.content:
One more time our development team delivered a fresh version with many updates and refinements. We recommend updating to 2.19.0.2 as soon as possible. If you’re already running a master–shadow setup, you can upgrade without downtime (follow the recommended high-availability upgrade path). The new build is available for download here.

What is new? As with previous updates, there are changes you’ll notice right away and others under the hood. Let’s take a quick tour—the complete list is in the release notes.

A few fixes to keep things smooth

  • System mappings via REST: You can now create entries with authenticationMode NONE_RESTRICTED using the API— andy when you don’t need principal propagation or a system-certificate logon.
  • Windows SNC helper scripts: The scripts for generating SNC configuration with the SAP Common Crypto Library now handle special characters in parameters and spaces in file names

 

Enhancements that make daily work easier

  • Broader platform coverage:
    • New OS and architecture support: Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 16, and macOS 26 (Tahoe). Linux on aarch64 is now supported. Check the Product Availability Matrix
    • Runtime update: SapMachine 25 can be used as the Java runtime. Check the Prerequisites
  • Simpler trust management:
    • Optional automatic trust synchronization makes it easier to use the automatic signing key rotation of SAP Authorization and Trust Management. How to configure trusted entities in Cloud Connector. Learn more: Trusted Entities Configuration and Keyrotation
  • Governance and roles:
    • Managed subaccounts : These can be modified only by users with an administrator role; other users get read-only access for safer operations at scale. 
    • New role: AssociateAdministrator —a lighter-weight admin role to delegate tasks while keeping guardrails in place. 
  • Connectivity options:
    • WebSocket RFC as an alternative to classic CPIC‑based RFC when talking to ABAP systems —useful in modern network environments. How to configure: can be found here.
  • Operations and observability:
    • Monitoring REST API for alerts: New endpoints to query and integrate alert information with your monitoring stack.
    • More configuration REST APIs: Endpoints for alerting, e-mail, subject pattern rules, and principal propagation validity—making policy and configuration changes scriptable. See configuration APIs. 
    • Troubleshooting improvements: Additional categories with likely causes and suggested fixes to speed up analysis when something goes wrong.

Upgrade checklist

  • Verify OS and architecture support in the Product Availability Matrix
  • Confirm your Java runtime meets prerequisites
  • Plan a master–shadow upgrade to avoid downtime and follow the recommended procedure.
  • Consider enabling automatic trust synchronization to simplify signing key rotation.
  • Review roles and governance: use AssociateAdministrator where suitable; adopt managed subaccounts.
  • Evaluate switching ABAP RFC traffic to WebSocket RFC if it better fits your landscape.

We’re excited about this release—broader platform choice, simpler trust, stronger governance, and improved visibility all help keep hybrid connectivity reliable and secure. Happy updating!

entry.title: SAP Developer News, March 19th, 2026
entry.content:
ITEMS 

ABAP Developer Challenge – halfway point! 

  • ABAP Developer Challenge - Joule for Developers using Basic Trial (blog post) https://community.sap.com/t5/abap-blog-posts/abap-developer-challenge-joule-for-developers-using-basic-trial/ba-p/14336191 

SAP Insider Las Vegas 

  • Event website https://vegas.sapinsider.org/  

Introducing SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition 

  • Introducing SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition (blog post) https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/introducing-sap-integration-suite-enhanced-edition/ba-p/14346638  

CAP Feb 2026 release 

  • Release notes https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/releases/2026/feb26  

Feedback for the btp CLI and Terraform providers 

  • Post with links to both surveys https://www.linkedin.com/posts/christian-lechner-inthecloud_do-you-use-the-btp-cli-andor-terraform-activity-7435231498376482816-rjWW/  

DYK #8 Orchestration Client in SAP Cloud SDK for AI 

  • SAP Cloud SDK for AI – Chat Completion documentation https://sap.github.io/ai-sdk/docs/js/orchestration/chat-completion  

CHAPTER TITLES  

00:00 Intro 
00:07 ABAP Developer Challenge – halfway point! 
00:39 SAP Insider Las Vegas 
01:59 Introducing SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition  
03:10 CAP Feb 2026 release 
04:58 Feedback for the btp CLI and Terraform providers  
05:56 DYK #8 Orchestration Client in SAP Cloud SDK for AI 

TRANSCRIPT

This is the SAP Developer News for March 19th, 2026.

[Sheena]
Hello, everyone. We are halfway through the March Developer Challenge on Joule
ABAP AI capabilities. And we would like to thank all of you for the amazing
responses we have been receiving for the past two weeks. As you might know,
week three is already live and we are focusing on RAP predict business logic to
create a sample code by passing a simple prompt. We have one more week
remaining and we are excited to hear even more great responses. Till then, stay
Jouled!

[Rich and Witalij]
Hey folks, it's Rich Heilman and Witalij here in Las Vegas for SAP Insider
2026. Today we did a two-hour hands-on session on Joule Studio, which was good.
Lots of good questions and tomorrow we're going to repeat that and we're going
to do a Joule for Developers in SAP Build and a Joule for Developers ABAP
edition as well where we'll show everyone how to use Joule to speed up their
development process. And you did a hands-on session this morning. Yes and I did
a session on how to use SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine Capabilities to build the
Joule augmented generation. Lots of people are talking about agents right now
but a lot of customers are doing RAG based AI applications so it was good to
refresh this content and as well to mention that we still keep investing in
some of the capabilities in SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine like for example the
very recent new encoder model. Cool very cool yeah so we got a couple more
hands-on sessions and I think we're gonna get out here and get on to the next
one. So, bye for now.

[Antonio]
Hola, SAP developers. There is a new edition available for SAP Integration
Suite, which includes lots of goodies. This is the new Enhanced Edition. From
an AI perspective, it includes features that were only previously available in
the Premium Edition. For example, iFlow generation using natural language,
script optimization to analyze, refactor, and optimize Groovy or JavaScript
scripts, API anomaly detection, and API traffic prediction to help you manage
your APIs. Apart from that, you also get access to the entire suite of
integration capabilities, an advanced event mesh broker for your event-driven
architecture scenarios, SAP Document AI to turn unstructured data into
structured data, the alert notification service for monitoring, transport
management service to move artifacts across landscapes without manual exports.
So, lots of stuff. Make sure to check out Tricia's blog post on SAP Community
to get all the details. Hasta luego. Hello, everyone.

[Ajay]
The February release of Capire documentation (*CAP) is out. First, we have an
exciting update. Live queries in the documentation. Yes, you have heard that
right. You can now run CDS queries directly in your browser. You can view the
query results along with the corresponding SQL statements. You can also edit
the query by typing in the box, making it your own personal playground to
experiment with, and no setups required. Coming to Node.js updates. Parallel
GET calls are now supported in the OData $batch request. For draft-enabled
entities, calculated elements can now be reliably used for values display in
the UI or for information in the UI behavior. Starting with @cap-js/sqlite
version Node.js SQL implementation by setting cds.requires.db.driver to node.
Coming to Java updates, important changes include using a reference as a value
for substring prefix or suffix in the contains startsWith or endsWith functions
is now rejected. Performance improvements have been made for requests related
to Fiori Draft list report "All" filter, deletion of inactive drafts during the
draft activation, and hierarchical selects. Tooling updates includes a new
query mode in cds REPL is available via the .ql command. CAP now supports
version 10 of ESLint. And for the Cloud Foundry, cds up can now be adjusted
using MTA extensions. For more details, visit the Capire documentation. Links
will be included in the description.

[DJ]
If you use the SAP Business Technology Platform, and more specifically, if
you're using the btp CLI or the Terraform providers to help you manage your
resources there, then your feedback is sought. The team has a couple of
feedback surveys open right now, running through April as well, so it's still a
little bit of time. One feedback survey for the btp CLI and the other for the
Terraform providers. This is a great opportunity for you to provide your
opinions and share with the team how you're using the tools and also where you
see room for improvement. Links to both surveys, which, as I mentioned, are
running sort of through April, are in the description. After this news episode
finishes, why not grab a coffee and head over and give your opinion right now.
Thanks.

[Kevin]
Did you know that you can configure an AI orchestration flow in AI Launchpad
and use it to configure the orchestration client from the SAP Cloud SDK for AI
within a CAP project? It allows you to configure templating, content filtering,
data masking, grounding, and more. You can download the configuration and read
it from file into your orchestration client or directly paste the JSON string
from AI Launchpad into your project. At any point, you can upload the
configuration back to AI Launchpad to make changes or directly change the
config inline of your project.

entry.title: Connecting SAP Integration Suite with Slack: A Hands‑On Guide with Open Connectors
entry.content:
Hello Everyone,

 

📝 Introduction:

In modern integration landscapes, real‑time notifications play a vital role in improving collaboration and transparency.
In this blog, I demonstrate how to send formatted messages from SAP Integration Suite to a Slack channel using the Slack Open Connector.

The integration flow is triggered through an HTTPS endpoint, where the received JSON payload is transformed using Groovy and HTML formatting, and then posted to Slack.
We also include an Exception Subprocess to handle invalid input or processing failures gracefully.

🔌Step 1: Connect to Slack via Open Connectors

To enable communication between SAP Integration Suite and Slack, start by creating a Slack instance in Open Connectors.

Steps:

  1. Navigate to Open Connectors under Extend Non-SAP Connectivity in the Home tab of your trial account.
  2. Search for Slack → Click Create Instance.
  3. Complete OAuth login for your Slack workspace.
  4. Capture the User, Organization and Element.
  5. Now got to the monitor tab in your trail account and create Credentials for Slack Open Connector under Manage Security Material.

     This allows SAP to securely access your Slack workspace.

⚙️Step 2: Build the Integration Flow (IFlow)

Now, create the IFlow that receives JSON input and posts a formatted message to Slack.

Components:

  • HTTPS Sender
  • Groovy Script
  • Open Connectors Adapter
  • Exception Subprocess

Steps:

  1. Add an HTTPS Sender configured for POST + JSON.
  2. Add a Groovy Script step to parse JSON and apply HTML tags for Slack.
  3. Connect Open Connectors Adapter  to the receiver and select Slack instance.
  4. Map the Groovy output to the Slack API request body.

 

This ensures the message sent to Slack is clean, structured, and readable.

🛑Step 3: Implement Exception Subprocess (Error Handling)

Add an Exception Subprocess to handle:

  • Token issues
  • Slack channel issues
  • Instance deleted
  • Slack connector invocation issues

Return a structured and meaningful error response.This improves reliability and debugging.

🧪 Step 4: Test the Flow Using Postman

Use Postman to trigger the HTTPS endpoint.

Here I used ATM Transaction as example JSON payload

Once posted, you should receive a nicely formatted Slack message in the selected channel.
 

 🚨 Step 5: See How It Works on Errors (Negative Tests)

I intentionally changed the Slack channelId to an incorrect value, which caused the Slack message delivery to fail. Because of this failure, the error‑handling subprocess was triggered, and I successfully received the error alert email — confirming that the error‑notification logic works as expected.
 

🏁Step 6: Conclusion

With HTTPS input, Groovy transformation, HTML styling, Slack Open Connector, and robust error handling, this setup provides a clean and efficient way to send notifications from SAP Integration Suite to Slack.
This approach can be extended to business workflows, alerts, or system updates.

If you have any questions or insights, feel free to share your thoughts in the comments!

@jareenabee_dudekula

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite - Client Authentication EKU deprecation
entry.content:
Authors: Adam Csallokozi, David Domokos

  • Let’s first determine your environment
  • Create Cloud Foundry Subaccount
  • 1. Create the CF Subaccount
  • 2. Add required data for the new CF Subaccount
  • 3. Enable Cloud Foundry
  • Verify the Destination Service
  • 1. Create the Destination Service Instance and Service Keys
  • 2. Generate the Private Key and a Certificate Signing Request (CSR)
  • 3. Generate the OAuth Token for Destination Service
  • 4. Generate the Certificate viaDestination Service REST API
  • 5. Retrieve the Newly Generated Certificate
  • Create and Load the Client Certificate into S/4HANA (STRUST)
  • Import the Client Certificate into SAP BTP
  • Cloud Integration running in NEO environment:
  • Cloud Integration in the Cloud Foundry environment:
  • Conclusion

Driven by an industry-wide change by Certification Authorities (see SAP KBA 3711618 – Client Authentication EKU Deprecation), newly procured publicly trusted SSL/TLS certificates may omit the “Client Authentication” Extended Key Usage (EKU) starting May 2026 as CAs separate server and client roles. For inbound scenarios that use Client Certificate Authentication (two‑way SSL/mTLS) with SAP Integration Suite, Cloud Integration, or API Management, the client certificate must include the Client Authentication EKU; certificates lacking this EKU will not be accepted by the Integration Suite endpoints.

There are multiple ways to implement this requirement. In this article,  we will demonstrate one of the three supported approaches, including the technical steps needed to generate and use the client certificate in your integration scenarios.

Let’s first determine your environment 

Before starting the implementation, first determine which environment applies to your landscape. The steps in this guide differ depending on the environment used for generating and signing the client certificate:

  • If you are working on Cloud Foundry environment please start with Verify the Destination Service chapter

  • If you are working on Neo environment please start with the Create Cloud Foundry Subaccount chapter

 

Create Cloud Foundry Subaccount

ℹ️ Please note that all steps described in this section can be performed free of charge and do not involve any additional costs ℹ️

⚠️ Please be aware to perform the listed actions you need to have a Global Account Admin role or appropriate admin colleague is required⚠️

If you are working on Neo environment, first create a new, Cloud Foundry (CF) Subaccount. This is required to use the Destination service to sign certificate.

1. Create the CF Subaccount

Go to your Global Account and click on 'Create' then choose 'Subaccount'

2. Add required data for the new CF Subaccount

 Please fill the required fields:

  • Display Name: Choose the desired name for your subaccount
  • Region: Choose the desired region for your subaccount

the rest of the fields will be filled automatically, after that click on 'Create'.

3. Enable Cloud Foundry

 

Once the Subaccount has been created, please open it. Click on “Enable Cloud Foundry” button. In the "Enable Cloud Foundry" popup window, input desired value, then press button "Create". Once see "Cloud Foundry Environment" section get populated with Cloud Foundry organization data, the Subaccount creation is successfully completed. 

ℹ️If you need more detailed guide to complete this step you can check our Help Page: Create a Subaccount ℹ️

Verify the Destination Service

In case you are running Cloud Integration / Integration Suite with CF environment, then please go to the CF subaccount where Cloud Integration / Integration Suite is subscribed. In case you are running Cloud Integration with NEO environment, then please go to the newly created CF subaccount as mentioned in chapter 'Create Cloud Foundry Subaccount'.  Then access  menu 'Entitlements' and confirm 'Destination Service' with service plan 'lite' is listed.

 

1. Create the Destination Service Instance and Service Keys

In BTP Cockpit, go to ‘Instances and Subscriptions’ on the left, then choose the ‘Create’ button in the top right corner

In the pop-up, set Service to Destination Service and Plan to lite. Select the Space where you have administrator permissions, then enter an Instance Name that follows your naming convention. Once the Create button is enabled, click Create.

⚠️When you enable Cloud Foundry (CF) for a new subaccount, only the org is created by default, no spaces are created. As a result, when you create a Destination Service instance, you may see a popup (as shown below) prompting you to create a space first ⚠️

  The instance is now created and visible on the Instances list.

Next, create a service key for the instance. Click the “lite” link to open the details panel on the right, navigate to the Service Keys section, and choose Create.

 

In the pop-up, enter a name for the Service Key, then click Create.

You should now see the newly created service key listed:

Finally, click View Credentials to display the generated credentials; you’ll use them in the next chapter.


 

2. Generate the Private Key and a Certificate Signing Request (CSR)

⚠️Command line (CMD) should be opened directly in the folder where the .KEY and .CSR files should be saved + OpenSSL needs to be installed on the computer ⚠️

To generate the private key and the CSR signed by a newly generated private key use openSSL. Execute the following command:

openssl req -utf8 -nodes -sha256 -newkey rsa:3072 -keyout genExample.key -out genExample.csr

⚠️All CSR attributes (shown below) will be overwritten during generation, so avoid spending too much time configuring them ⚠️

The command generates genExample.key (private key). You won’t need it for the API steps, but keep it for later use (for example, when creating a .PFX). As a last step of this chapter, the generated .CSR file needs to be Base64‑encoded. This can be done (in case of Windows operating system) via Windows Powershell.

Open Windows PowerShell in the folder containing genExample.csr and run the command:

[Convert]::ToBase64String([IO.File]::ReadAllBytes("genExample.csr"))

as a result the Base64 encoded value of the .CSR file will be displayed in the PowerShell window:

Please copy that value and save it because it will be needed in a later step, you can save it as simple text file in the same folder as the previously created files.

3. Generate the OAuth Token for Destination Service

In this step the OAuth token will be generated that is needed for the certificate creation. To retrieve that OAuth token, either follow the API documentation, or download our predefined REST API collection from here. This collection will be used for the rest if the steps in the 4th and 5th chapters.

Open the “DestinationServiceTokenGeneration” request and populate its variables with the actual values from the credentials you created in the last step of Chapter 1 (Create the Destination Service Instance and Service Keys).

Collection VariableActual Value from Credentials
host (in the URL section)url (https://XXXX.authentication.eu12.hana.ondemand.com)
clientID (in the Body section)clientid (sb-clone9ae33f841f774443XXXXXXXXXXXXX)
clientSecret (in the Body section)clientsecret (1c8d712b-b4ed-42ff-bb17-c2c8db2e166b$ROCeUoa1GXXXXXXXXXXXXX)

After you send the request with your actual values, the response body will include the OAuth token:

 

4. Generate the Certificate via Destination Service REST API

From the collection, open the request 'DestinationServiceCertificateGenerator'

Collection VariableActual Value from Credentials
host (in the URL section)uri (https://destination-configuration.cfapps.XXXXXXX.ondemand.com)
Name (in payload of the body)Desired name of the created certificate (TestCert)
CN_value (in payload of the body)Common Name, represents the primary, specific domain name (MyDomain)
Base64_Encoded_CSR (in payload of the body)Paste the Base64 encoded value of .CSR that is created as a last step in the chapter '2. Generate the Private Key and a Certificate Signing Request (CSR)'
BearerToken (in Authorization Header)Value of the token that is generated in the previous step (eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJqaWQiOiJoKys5MWZsblpnVXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX)

In the response of the API call the HTTP 201 - Created should be appear as response code:

 

5. Retrieve the Newly Generated Certificate

From the collection, open the request 'FetchNewCertificate' and a last step retrieve the created certificate chain.

Collection VariableActual Value from Credentials
host (in the URL section)uri (https://destination-configuration.cfapps.XXXXXXX.ondemand.com)
name (in the URL section)In the previous step created name of the certificate (TestCert)
BearerToken (in Authorization Header)Value of the token that is generated in the previous step (eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJqaWQiOiJoKys5MWZsblpnVXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX)

The API should return HTTP 200 OK, and the payload contains the newly created certificate chain, this can be found in the 'Content' field:

Save this value in a text file (base64.txt), because it is needed to Base64-decode the value to get the certificate chain. To perform the Base64 decoding open Windows PowerShell in the folder containing the recently saved file and run the command:

$base64 = Get-Content base64.txt -Raw
[IO.File]::WriteAllBytes("certChain.txt", [Convert]::FromBase64String($base64))

This command will:

  • read the Base64 string from base64.txt
  • Convert it to bytes
  • Write the bytes to certChain.txt in the folder where the PowerShell was opened

Once the command completes, verify that the created file contains content like:

You can then save it as a .PEM file and generate a .PFX for client certificate based authentication, by referring to the blog “Client Certificate Authentication in SAP Cloud Integration: Configuration and Best Practices,” section 1.1/A, “Generating the Client Certificate (.PFX file) – Using OpenSSL.”

ℹ️BTP Connectivity Service on CN data centers signs the keypair using [CDC Cloud Root CA] ℹ️

Create and Load the Client Certificate into S/4HANA (STRUST)

This section explains how to create and load a client certificate in S/4HANA using transaction STRUST. If you’re connecting from S/4HANA, you can generate the CSR directly in STRUST - no OpenSSL needed. After generating the CSR, use the Destination Service API to sign it and obtain the client certificate, then import the signed certificate back into the client PSE in STRUST for client certificate–based authentication.

 

  1. Create an SSL client PSE in S/4HANA; for exact steps refer to 2. Configuration Steps in the Backend 

  2. Right‑click the PSE and choose Create. (Make sure you are in Change mode.)

  3. Generate new Client Certificate and sign the certificate: STRUST -> Edit -> Create certificate request.

  4. In the popup, select Use PSE algorithm as the Signature algorithm, and press Continue.

  5. Save the signing request, which should have the following format:
    -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE REQUEST-----
    <CSR>
    -----END CERTIFICATE REQUEST-----

  6. Encode the CSR in Base64 (the Destination Service API expects a Base64 string without the PEM headers/footers).

  7. In the DestinationServiceCertificateGeneration request, copy the encoded CSR in the body in the parameter "CSR".

  8. After the certificate is signed by the Destination Service API, Base64‑decode it, then in STRUST choose Edit -> Import certificate response. Import the full certificate chain (from the decoded file).

Import the Client Certificate into SAP BTP

Cloud Integration running in NEO environment:

In Neo, inbound client-certificate authentication is configured by uploading the caller’s public certificate and mapping it to a platform user. This is done in the Certificate-to-User Mapping monitor in the Operations view.

  1. Open your Cloud Integration tenant and switch to Operations.
  2. Go to Manage Security > Certificate-to-User Mapping.
  3. Click Add and upload the client’s public certificate file (.cer). Upload only the leaf certificate of the client, not the chain.
  4. Assign a user name to this certificate. Use the technical platform user that should run the iFlow.
  5. Save the mapping.

Cloud Integration in the Cloud Foundry environment:

To make the client certificate available to your Integration flows, create an external-certificate service key:

  1. Go to the SAP BTP cockpit, open your subaccount, and navigate to Instances and Subscriptions
  2. Select your desired 'Process Integration Runtime' with plan 'integration-flow' service instance
  3. Choose Create under Service Keys
  4. Enter a name for the service key
  5. Set Key Type to External Certificate and paste the PEM-encoded client certificate (only the first/leaf certificate, not the chain) into the External Certificate field
  6. Select Create

For more detailed steps, refer to the KBA 3297437  - Client Cert Authentication for Inbound calls to CPI tenants on CF.

Conclusion

The upcoming EKU deprecation underscores the need to proactively adjust certificate management practices in SAP Integration Suite landscapes. By implementing the recommended steps, organizations can maintain secure, compliant client certificate authentication for inbound scenarios. Preparing early helps prevent disruptions and ensures integrations continue to run smoothly as industry standards evolve.

entry.title: Accelerating SAP ICSM and IRT integration with pre-packaged sample content
entry.content:
Introduction

Integrating SAP Intelligent Clinical Supply Management (ICSM) with IRT/RTSM systems is essential for running efficient clinical trials but many teams still end up reinventing the same integration patterns.

To help the ecosystem move faster, sample integration content for ICSM is now available on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub, providing a practical starting point for building integrations between ICSM and IRT systems using SAP Integration Suite.

This post introduces the content, highlights the supported ICSM-IRT scenarios and explains how partners and customers can reuse and extend these building blocks to accelerate real‑world integrations.

Business value

Instead of starting from scratch, partners and customer can now:

  • Reuse proven integration patterns
  • Accelerate ICSM-IRT implementations
  • Focus on value‑adding extensions rather than plumbing
  • Align with SAP‑recommended architecture and standards

The content is intentionally modular, making it easier to adapt to different IRT systems or extend with additional scenarios.

What's Included

The published package SAP Intelligent Clinical Supply Management-Sample Integration with IRT demonstrates end-to-end integration scenarios across both planning and operations, based on GS1 Clinical Trials standards (3.6) and SAP-recommended integration patterns.

How to Consume the Accelerator Content

This can be consumed using SAP’s standard process for pre-packaged integration content: The pre-packaged integration content in SAP integration Suite is a set of Integration flows (iflows) leveraging the capabilities of SAP Integration Suite and SAP BTP to integrate IRT and SAP ICSM smoothly. This prepackage content takes care of many aspects of integration; some of these are as follows:

  1. Connect the IRT system to SAP Integration Suite artifacts via API management.
  2. Transformation of message payload from GS1 to ICSM and vice versa.
  3. Seamless integration of SAP Event Mesh to take an action against the events raised from ICSM (e.g. sales order created; Inventory report executed etc.).

This pre-package integration content supports the following interfaces for planning and operation in ICSM:

Interfaces for ICSM Planning

  1. Study Information
  2. IRT actuals and Master

Interfaces for ICSM Operations

  1. Inventory Report
  2. Shipment request and confirmation
  3. Dispatch Advice
  4. Receiving Advice
  5. Dispensation Advice
  6. Kit Status Change (Inbound/Outbound)

Below are the steps to consume the pre-packaged integration content:

Step 1 - Discover the package

Navigate to your SAP Integration Suite - Integration - Discover tab and search for the package "SAP Intelligent Clinical Supply Management Integration with IRT"

Below is the diagram shows the navigation steps.

                                                                                                              Fig: Integration Suite

Select the package and click copy to move it into your design workspace.  

Note: As with all standard packages, once you copy it, modifications in your workplace do not auto-inherit future SAP updates- SAP recommends keeping custom changes separate.

Step 2 - Review the Artifacts

Review each artifact integration flows and configure the user credentials for ICSM APIs and OAuth2 Client Credentials for event mesh using its service key values.

Step 3 - Configure the defined externalized parameters in each artifact

Update the values in each of the defined externalized parameters in each of the artifact integration flows.

The integration flows combine API-based and Event-driven approaches using SAP Integration Suite, SAP API Management, SAP Event Mesh and Cloud Connector, reflecting a reference architecture suitable for real-world landscapes.

Reference, not a Black Box

This is sample/accelerator content. Its purpose is to illustrate integration approaches and provide reusable building blocks - not to replace product features.

As with other SAP community assets, it does not come with the same lifecycle guarantees or support rigor as standard SAP product functionality. Instead, it is meant to enable the ecosystem to innovate, customize, and deliver differentiated solutions on top of SAP ICSM.

Get Started

  • Explore the sample Integration Package on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub.
  • Review the ICSM-IRT Integration architecture and patterns.
  • Copy, adapt and extend the content for your own scenarios.

If you are an IRT vendor, Partner or Customer working with ICSM, then this is an opportunity to reduce the integration effort and contribute to a growing, interoperable ecosystem around clinical supply management.

This Accelerator Content lays out the groundwork for real-world ICSM-IRT integrations by providing pre-built, GS1 aligned, event driven flows that can be directly adopted for the proof-of-concept work. 

In the upcoming blog of this series, this accelerator content forms the technical backbone for validating the end-to-end data exchange between ICSM and an IRT platform, which accelerates onboarding IRT partners and helps to reduce the custom development effort.

Engage with us

If you are interested in learning more about SAP Intelligent Clinical Supply Management, please consider following the new "SAP Intelligent Clinical Supply Management".

We are happy to receive your feedback, questions or thoughts about SAP Intelligent Clinical Supply Management in a comment.

entry.title: Unlock Enterprise-Scale Integration and Automation with SAP: A 5-Part Webcast Series
entry.content:
Running integration at enterprise scale requires more than connecting systems - it demands a unified strategy, consistent governance, and a strong operational foundation. To help organizations navigate this complex landscape, we’re excited to announce a deep-dive 5-part webcast series that shows how SAP Integration Suite and SAP Business Technology Platform can serve as a true enterprise-wide integration platform.

Whether you’re looking to simplify complexity, strengthen governance, or enable future-ready automation, this series provides a structured path from vision to sustainable execution. Here’s a breakdown of what you can expect from each session:

 

1. Rethinking Integration: Building a Unified Enterprise-wide Platform

Integration starts with consolidation and unification. This session explores how organizations can position SAP Integration Suite as a central platform, connecting systems, processes, and data across the enterprise. Learn strategies to streamline your integration landscape and create a foundation for scalable, enterprise-wide operations.

Register here:  https://event.on24.com/eventRegistration/EventLobbyServlet?target=reg20.jsp&eventid=5256733&sessionid=1&key=2F2D713929E44EC97A0255F0674D0915&groupId=6661068&sourcepage=register

 

2. From Vision to Execution: Building a Scalable Integration Strategy

Turning integration vision into reality requires a deliberate, structured approach. This session dives into the steps organizations can take to develop a scalable integration strategy that aligns business goals with technology execution. Discover best practices for governance, architecture, and execution that drive measurable value.

Register here: https://event.on24.com/eventRegistration/EventLobbyServlet?target=reg20.jsp&eventid=5256733&sessionid=1&key=2F2D713929E44EC97A0255F0674D0915&groupId=6661111&sourcepage=register

 

3. Achieve Enterprise-grade Operations for SAP Integration Suite

Operational excellence is critical to sustaining integration at scale. In this session, we focus on how to strengthen governance, monitor performance, and build resilient processes with SAP Integration Suite. Learn how enterprise-grade operations ensure consistency, reliability, and scalability across your integration landscape.

Regiser here: https://event.on24.com/eventRegistration/EventLobbyServlet?target=reg20.jsp&eventid=5259511&sessionid=1&key=905F0343E0BA503A283C97B3645BCB31&groupId=6667225&sourcepage=register

 

4. Drive Intelligent Business Transformation with Agentic Enterprise Automation

Intelligent business transformation requires more than isolated automation - it demands connected, adaptive processes powered by AI. Explore how SAP Business Technology Platform and SAP Business Transformation Management enable agentic enterprise automation at scale. Learn how to design, govern, and measure AI agents that drive true process transformation, generating measurable outcomes across complex enterprise environments.

Register here: https://event.on24.com/eventRegistration/EventLobbyServlet?target=reg20.jsp&eventid=5259684&sessionid=1&key=10935CA2537B96624DD065F7EA514C2F&groupId=6661382&sourcepage=register

 

5. Develop Robust Automations with SAP Build and SAP Integration Suite

Agentic enterprise automation only succeeds when systems, workflows, and applications are seamlessly connected. This session demonstrates how SAP Build and SAP Integration Suite work together to enable end-to-end automation across SAP and non-SAP landscapes. See live demos, customer success stories, and practical examples showing how low-code development and enterprise-grade integration accelerate automation while maintaining control, visibility, and scalability.

Register here: https://event.on24.com/eventRegistration/EventLobbyServlet?target=reg20.jsp&eventid=5258498&sessionid=1&key=76AD4CD903C206A20A2CFFE8270CC25C&groupId=6661286&sourcepage=register

 

This series is designed for integration architects, IT leaders, and business transformation professionals who want to take their enterprise integration and automation initiatives to the next level. Join us to learn how SAP can help you simplify complexity, achieve operational excellence, and unlock intelligent, future-ready automation.

Save your spot 🙂

entry.title: Virtual Landscape Stages for Cloud Integration Pipeline
entry.content:
We are excited to announce the release of version 1.0.4 of the Cloud Integration Pipeline package. This update introduces several key innovations, including the support for Virtual Backend Landscape Stages managed within a single Pipeline package and a single Integration Suite tenant.

Introduction & Motivation

This feature evolves the landscape support first introduced in version 1.0.9 of our legacy (now deprecated) all-in-one Process Integration Pipeline package. Previously, the pipeline allowed you to run scenarios across different stages without altering Partner Directory configurations - such as XSLTs for receiver determination. It achieved this by mapping the actual Sender Business System name to a "Sender Alias" based on the tenant's runtime environment.

The Challenge: The 1:1 Limitation

While the previous model worked seamlessly for 1:1 landscapes (e.g., one DEV tenant for one DEV backend), it struggled with multiple backend landscapes in one tenant. A common challenge arises when a single non-production SAP Integration Suite tenant is used to integrate both Development and Quality Assurance backends (e.g., S/4HANA).

In such cases, the landscape stage was "locked" to the tenant. You could define the stage for S/4HANA DEV or S/4HANA QA, but not both simultaneously. This forced integrators into a workaround: one system would have to be defined with its actual technical name (e.g., S4DCLNT100) in the Partner Directory, breaking the clean abstraction that the pipeline aims to provide.

The Solution: Virtual Stages

The new Virtual Stages feature is designed specifically for non-productive tenants where a single integration scenario must serve multiple landscape stages of the same backend.

Historically, achieving this in SAP Process Orchestration (PO) was also cumbersome. Typical approaches:

  • Backend Inbound Scenarios: Creating "dummy" XPath conditions in Receiver Determinations to switch between receivers.
  • Backend Outbound Scenarios: Duplicating Integrated Configurations (ICOs) for every actual sender.

The Cloud Integration Pipeline now provides an elegant, single-package solution for these requirements. In the next increments it is planned to add an option to define Integration Scenario in the context of a stage (e.g. if Scenario changed in Development stage but still old version is used in Quality).

Core Use Cases

We have optimized this framework to cover three primary architectural scenarios:

  1. Consolidated Non-Prod Environments: Supporting a 3-tier backend landscape using a 2-tier Integration Suite setup. This allows you to manage multiple backend stages through one pipeline in a single tenant.
  2. Dual-Track Backend Support: SAP S/4HANA landscapes often utilize "Maintenance" and "Project" tracks. Maintaining a dual-track middleware landscape to match this is a significant overhead. Virtual Stages allow you to support track-based routing for the backend using a single middleware instance.
  3. Cross-Stage Multicasting: More exotic use case - you may need to route a single message from a sender to multiple stages of the same receiver (e.g., sending data to both MDG-DEV and MDG-QA simultaneously). Since the Receiver Alias (MDG) remains constant, the pipeline now supports multicasting across virtual stages without requiring extra routing branches or modifications to Receiver Determination rules (they can be kept the same as in Production tenant).

Leveraging Authenticated Users for Dynamic Inbound Processing

Integration scenarios triggered from external sources (push-based) carry the SapAuthenticatedUserName header (available if it is added to the IFlow’s "Allowed Headers" list). This header contains the Client ID used by the caller. In SAP Integration Suite, a unique Client ID is generated for every Service Instance created via the BTP Cockpit.

Historically, the Pipeline determined the Scenario ID using the SAP_Sender (system name) and SAP_SenderInterface (interface name) headers. While effective, this model makes it difficult to build truly generic inbound processing steps for certain adapters.

The Challenge with Generic Inbound Adapters

Determining a sender interface dynamically is relatively straightforward (e.g., using XPath on an XML root or parsing the URI path in an HTTPS adapter). However, identifying the Sender System is much harder when that information isn't part of the payload or provided by standard adapter headers.

A prime example is the SOAP/SAP RM adapter. It lacks standard headers to identify the sender system and does not provide a unique URI path for the called service. Without a static header, dynamic sender determination becomes a technical hurdle.

The Solution: Identity-Based Determination

By supporting the SapAuthenticatedUserName header, the Pipeline can now implement Identity-Based Sender Determination. Instead of relying on manually populated headers (e.g. from externalized parameters), the Pipeline resolves the sender by mapping the caller's Client ID to a specific system or context in the Partner Directory.

This not only simplifies generic IFlow design but also provides a foundation for the Virtual Stages functionality. Two primary configuration patterns are now supported:

  1. Landscape Stage Resolution: Map an Authenticated User to a Partner ID using the Landscape_Stage~ prefix in the Partner Directory. This allows you to automatically identify the landscape stage (e.g., DEV, QA) across all scenarios based on which service key the sender uses.
  2. System-Specific Mapping: Map an Authenticated User directly to a Business System Partner ID. This identifies the exact business system via the caller's identity, effectively replacing the need for a static SAP_Sender header across multiple sender systems.

Architecture Overview

A single Integration Suite tenant can now host multiple parallel "stages" for your business systems (e.g., QA~Project, QA~Maintenance, Pre-Production, etc.).

To map your tenant to these Landscape Stages (also known as Virtual Stages), you can choose between two approaches:

  • Structured Assignment: Explicitly assign all possible virtual stages to the Partner SAP_Integration_Suite_Landscape in the Partner Directory.
  • Dynamic Promotion: If a stage is supplied at runtime - via a header or the Authenticated User approach - that differs from the global configuration, the pipeline "promotes" this value into the current context.

The only prerequisite is that these Virtual Stages must be maintained in the Partner Directory for the respective Sender or Receiver System Aliases.

Core Logic: Identity & Scenario Resolution

The primary goal of the pipeline is to determine the Partner ID of an Integration Scenario. Traditionally, this relies on the SAP_Sender (Sender System name that is checked for System Alias existence) and SAP_SenderInterface headers. We have extended this logic to resolve the Virtual Stage and Sender Context before identifying the Scenario ID.

After running this resolution logic, the landscape stage could remain ambiguous (exact stage is unknown) - in this case multiple possible stages are transferred in the header tenantStage and the pipeline will try to navigate the required routing logic in the Receiver Stage determination.

The virtual stage routing follows a specific guardrail: the logic only executes if the header tenantStageVirtual is set to 'true'. This ensures that virtualization overhead never affects Production tenants. During Receiver Stage discovery, the system evaluates the target landscape. If the routing rules identify multiple valid virtual stages for a single Receiver Alias (only possible for Asynchronous processing), a Message Split occurs. The source message is then multicasted to all determined stages (e.g., both a Maintenance and a Project track). If only a single stage is identified, the tenantStage header is updated, and the message proceeds without multicasting.

Receiver Stage Discovery

The discovery process depends heavily on whether the Tenant Stage is already known and whether the Receiver is defined via a System Alias (abstraction) rather than a physical Business System.

Receiver Landscape Stage makes only sense for Receivers with maintained System Alias:

Scenario 1: The Tenant Stage is Known

When the Stage is identified, the system follows the 5-Step Routing Hierarchy:

  • Stage-Specific Rule Override: Use a specific rule to redirect the "Natural Choice" to a different stage. Stage-Specific Rule can be defined at the Configuration Scenario or at Receiver Alias level.
  • Natural Choice: By default, route to the identical stage of the receiver (e.g., DEV to DEV).
  • Tenant-Level Fallback Rules: A Tenant-Wide rule maintained at the Configuration Scenario or at Receiver Alias level, independent of the specific stage.

Scenario 2: The Tenant Stage is Unknown (Ambiguous)

If the Stage is unknown, the "Natural Choice" is unavailable. The system then evaluates:

  • Tenant-Level Rules: A Tenant-Wide rule maintained at the Configuration Scenario or at Receiver Alias level, independent of the specific stage.
  • Deterministic Exit: If the Receiver Alias has only one possible stage, the system auto-routes to that single destination.

To implement these rules, you must maintain String parameters in the Partner Directory using the following naming convention:

  • Parameter ID: stageRule_ + {Stage Name OR Tenant Name} + optional ~{Receiver Alias}.
  • Value Syntax: The character > followed by a comma-separated list of target stages.

Examples:

  • Stage-Specific with Receiver Alias: stageRule_DEV-MNT~S4HANA with value >DEV,QA-PRJ
  • Tenant-Wide without Receiver Alias: stageRule_My-IS-Tenant with value >QA-MNT

For a deep dive into the syntax and advanced configuration options, please refer to the Landscape Stages.

Sample Scenarios

In the following, we show you how to setup flexible stages and stage rules based on a couple of sample scenarios. Much more options are supported than shown here. For more details about all the options, see Landscape Stages.

As a prerequisite, we first need to create a partner ID SAP_Integration_Suite_Landscape in the Partner Directory where we maintain the list off all virtual stages as shown below. As you can see, the DEV tenant is mapped to two stages whereas the QA tenant is mapped to 3 stages. For PRD, we do not have virtual stages, here we have a 1:1 mapping.

Scenario passing message header to pipeline

In the first scenario, we simply pass the virtual stage via the message header tenantStage from the scenario-specific inbound processing flow to the generic flows of the pipeline. Furthermore, we have defined the sender system name, the sender interface name as well as the sender interface namespace in the scenario-specific inbound processing flow which is used to determine the configuration scenario. As you can see, we have externalized all parameters so that you can configure the parameters without need of changing the flow.

Because the stage name is passed via the message header, no other check is carried out to determine the stage.
